title: T.Karuppusamy vs K.Dhanalakshmi @ Thangamani on 10 April, 2019
body: T.Karuppusamy vs K.Dhanalakshmi @ Thangamani on 10 April, 2019 Author: V.Bharathidasan Bench: V.Bharathidasan 1 IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT MADRAS DATED : 10.04.2019 CORAM THE HONOURABLE MR.JUSTICE V.BHARATHIDASAN S.A. 445 of 2019 and C.M.P. 7110 of 2019 T.Karuppusamy .. Appellant Vs 1. K.Dhanalakshmi @ Thangamani 2. T.Rajendran 3. M.Thirumalaisamy @ Kuppusamy .. Respondents PRAYER :- This Second Appeal has been filed under Section 100 of C.P.C. against the judgement and decree passed in A.S.No.114 of 2012 on the file of the learned Principal Subordinate Judge, Coimbatore, dated 02.04.2014 reversing the decree and judgement made in O.S.No.113 of 2005 on the file of the learned District Munsif, Mettupalayam dated 14.02.2012. For Appellants : Mr.S.Selvathirumurugan JUDGMENT The 1st defendant is the appellant herein. The 1st respondent/plaintiff is the wife of the appellant filed a suit for maintenance. The Trial Court dismissed the suit. Challenging the http://www.judis.nic.in 2 same, the 1st respondent wife has filed an appeal in A.S.No.114 of 2012, and the lower appellate court has reversed the judgement and decree passed by the Trial Court, and allowed the appeal, and thereby decreed the suit and granted a sum of Rs.1000/- as maintenance. Challenging the judgement and decree, the 1st respondent husband has filed the present Second Appeal. 2. The case of the plaintiff is that the plaintiff is the legally wedded wife of 1st defendant, and the marriage took place on 11.04.1990 at Pollachi, and even prior to the marriage, the mother of plaintiff one Valliammal has transferred her property in favour of 1st defendant under a sale deed dated 02.03.1990. After the marriage, both plaintiff and the 1st defendant lived together for about 2½ years at Pollachi. Thereafter, the 1st defendant has demanded dowry and the plaintiff was driven out from the matrimonial home, and residing at her parental house. Earlier, she has filed an application for maintenance under Sec.125 of Crl.P.C. on the file of the Judicial Magistrate, Mettupalayam in M.C.No.8 of 1993, and that application has been dismissed. The plaintiff is not in a position to maintain her and the 1st defendant has wilfully failed to pay maintenance. In http://www.judis.nic.in 3 the above circumstances, she has filed the suit claiming maintenance. 3. The 1st defendant filed a written statement admitting the marriage between the plaintiff and the 1st defendant, and denied the allegation of demanding dowry. So far as the execution of sale deed is concerned, the property has been sold on payment of consideration, and only after the marriage, the 1st defendant has come to know about the plaintiff's ill-health, and mental immaturity, and however, he has been insisted to desert the plaintiff and live separately. After some time, mother and brother of plaintiff have demanded the 1st defendant to sell the property to them, and when he refused to do so, there was a quarrel between them and for which, a police complaint was also lodged by the 1st defendant. Thereafter, the 1st defendant was forced to withdraw the complaint, and enraged by the refusal of the 1st defendant, the plaintiff quarrelled with the 1st defendant and left matrimonial house. Thereafter, she has filed an application under Sec. 125 of Crl.P.C. seeking maintenance and that was ended in dismissal. Now, she is not entitled for the maintenance. The 1st defendant has also no means to pay maintenance. http://www.judis.nic.in 4 4. The Trial Court dismissed the suit holding that, the plaintiff's suit for maintenance under Sec. 125 of Crl.P.C. came to be dismissed by the Judicial Magistrate, Pollachi. Hence, the plaintiff cannot file a suit seeking for maintenance. That apart, there is no evidence to show that the 1st defendant husband has deserted his wife. Challenging the same, the plaintiff has filed an appeal. The lower appellate court after considering the materials came to a conclusion that earlier, the petition filed under Sec.125 of Crl.P.C. is not a bar for seeking maintenance under Hindu Adoption and Maintenance Act, and that apart, there is no clear evidence that the plaintiff has deserted the 1st defendant and it is only the 1st defendant has deserted the plaintiff, and allowed the appeal, thereby granted past maintenance at the rate of Rs.10000/- and future maintenance of Rs.1000/- per month and the charge is created over a sum of Rs.44,000/- lying in the court deposit. Now, challenging the same, the present Second Appeal has been filed. 5. In the grounds of appeal, the following question of law have been raised by the appellant :- http://www.judis.nic.in 5 a) Whether the suit is barred by filing a petition under Sec.18(2) of Hindu Adoption and Maintenance Act. b) Whether the suit is not barred in view of the dismissal of application filed under Sec.125 of Crl.P.C. 6. I have considered the submissions made by Mr.S.Selvathirumurugan, learned counsel appearing for the appellant and perused the relevant records carefully. 7. It is an admitted fact that the plaintiff is legally wedded wife of appellant/1st defendant. It is contended by the plaintiff that it is only the 1st defendant has deserted her and she is entitled for maintenance, but, the 1st defendant only relying upon the order passed under Sec.125 of Crl.P.C., wherein the earlier application filed by the plaintiff for maintenance was dismissed. It is settled position that dismissal of application filed under Sec.125 of Crl.P.C. would not be bar for filing a suit seeking maintenance under Sec. 18 of Hindu Adoption and Maintenance Act. That apart, the materials would show that the plaintiff is living separately, and there is no evidence to show that the plaintiff has deserted the 1st defendant and living http://www.judis.nic.in 6 separately. Considering all those facts and materials, the first appellate court has decreed the suit, and granted maintenance of Rs.1000/- per month. I have also gone through the materials available on record, I do not find any infirmity, illegality or perversity in the judgement passed by the first appellate court, and I find no reason to interfere with the judgement and decree passed by the first appellate court. Hence, there is no substantial question of law arises for consideration in the present second appeal. In the result, the Second Appeal stands dismissed. No costs. Consequently, the C.M.P. is closed. 10.04.2019 Index : Yes/No Internet: Yes/No Speaking/Non Speaking order rpp To Principal Subordinate Judge, Coimbatore. http://www.judis.nic.in 7 V.BHARATHIDASAN, J. rpp S.A. 445 of 2019 and C.M.P. 7110 of 2019 10.04.2019 http://www.judis.nic.in
